title: Find errors from a specific source
prompt: Write an SPL search that finds all events with `sourcetype=app_log` containing the word "error" in the last 24 hours.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=app_log "error" earliest=-24h

explanation: SPL searches start with filter terms; `sourcetype=` narrows to a specific log type, a bare quoted string does a full-text match, and `earliest=-24h` bounds the time range relative to now.

title: Count events per host
prompt: Write an SPL query to count the number of events per `host` for `sourcetype=access_combined`.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | stats count by host

explanation: `stats count by host` is the SPL equivalent of `GROUP BY` + `COUNT(*)` in SQL — it groups matching events by the `host` field and counts how many fall into each group.

title: Show only two fields
prompt: Write a query that searches `sourcetype=access_combined` and displays only the `clientip` and `status` fields.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | table clientip, status

explanation: `| table` restricts the output to the named fields in the order given, which is the simplest way to trim a wide event down to just what you need to see.

title: Top 5 status codes
prompt: Write an SPL query that shows the 5 most common HTTP `status` codes in `sourcetype=access_combined`.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | top limit=5 status

explanation: `top` automatically counts occurrences of a field's values, sorts descending, and computes percentage of total — `limit=5` caps it to the top 5 rows, saving you from writing `stats count by status | sort -count | head 5` by hand.

title: Most recent 10 failed logins
prompt: Write a query that finds events where `action=failure` in `sourcetype=auth_log` and shows the 10 most recent ones.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=auth_log action=failure | sort -_time | head 10

explanation: `_time` is Splunk's internal timestamp field; sorting by `-_time` puts the newest events first, and `head 10` keeps only the first 10 rows of that sorted result.

title: Extract an order ID with rex
prompt: Given events containing the text `order_id=12345`, write a query to extract the numeric order ID into a field called `orderId`.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=orders | rex field=_raw "order_id=(?<orderId>\d+)"

explanation: `rex` applies a regular expression to a field (here `_raw`, the full raw event text) and any named capture group — `(?<orderId>...)` — becomes a new searchable field on the fly, without needing a permanent field extraction defined.

title: Requests per 5-minute interval
prompt: Write an SPL query that charts the count of events for `sourcetype=access_combined` in 5-minute buckets over the last 4 hours.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined earliest=-4h | timechart span=5m count

explanation: `timechart` buckets events into time intervals set by `span=` and computes an aggregate (here `count`) per bucket — the standard way to build a time-series trend chart in Splunk.

title: Flag slow requests
prompt: Given events with a `response_time` field in milliseconds, write a query that adds a field `is_slow` set to "yes" when `response_time` exceeds 2000, otherwise "no".

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | eval is_slow=if(response_time>2000, "yes", "no")

explanation: `eval` creates or overwrites a field using an expression evaluated per event; `if(condition, true_value, false_value)` is SPL's inline conditional, directly analogous to a `CASE WHEN` in SQL.

title: Average and max response time per endpoint
prompt: Write a query that shows, for each `uri_path`, the average and maximum `response_time`.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | stats avg(response_time) AS avg_rt, max(response_time) AS max_rt by uri_path

explanation: `stats` accepts multiple aggregate functions in one pass, each aliased with `AS` for a readable column name, all grouped `by uri_path` in a single, efficient scan of the data.

title: Hosts with more errors than a threshold
prompt: Write a query that lists hosts where the count of `level=ERROR` events exceeds 100 in the last 24 hours.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=app_log level=ERROR earliest=-24h | stats count by host | where count > 100

explanation: `stats` first computes the per-host error count, then the pipe into `where count > 100` filters that aggregated result — equivalent to a `HAVING` clause in SQL, since `where` here runs on already-summarized data.

title: Enrich events with a lookup table
prompt: Given a lookup table `department_lookup.csv` mapping `employee_id` to `department`, write a query that enriches `sourcetype=badge_swipes` events with the department name.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=badge_swipes | lookup department_lookup.csv employee_id OUTPUT department

explanation: `lookup` joins each event to a matching row in the CSV lookup table on the shared `employee_id` field and adds the specified `OUTPUT` field(s) — Splunk's equivalent of a SQL `JOIN` against a small reference table.

title: Group multi-event sessions
prompt: Write a query that groups `sourcetype=web_events` into sessions by `session_id`, where a new session starts if there's a gap of more than 30 minutes between events.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=web_events | transaction session_id maxpause=30m

explanation: `transaction` stitches related events together into a single multi-value "transaction" event grouped by the given field(s); `maxpause=30m` splits the grouping whenever the gap between consecutive events for the same `session_id` exceeds 30 minutes.

title: Percent of daily traffic per host
prompt: Write a query that, for each event in `sourcetype=access_combined` today, computes what percentage of the day's total events came from that event's `host`.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=access_combined earliest=@d
| eventstats count AS total
| stats count AS host_count by host, total
| eval pct=round((host_count/total)*100, 2)

explanation: `eventstats` computes an aggregate (`total` events overall) and attaches that same value back onto every event without collapsing rows, unlike `stats`. This lets a later `stats ... by host` compute per-host counts while `total` is still available for the percentage `eval`.

title: Detect brute-force login attempts
prompt: Write an SPL query that flags any source IP with 5 or more failed logins (`action=failure`) within a 10-minute window in `sourcetype=auth_log`.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=auth_log action=failure
| bin _time span=10m
| stats count by src_ip, _time
| where count >= 5

explanation: `bin _time span=10m` rounds each event's timestamp down into 10-minute buckets, so grouping `by src_ip, _time` afterward effectively counts failures per IP *per 10-minute window* rather than across the whole search range — the core pattern behind Splunk correlation/detection searches.

title: Optimize a slow wildcard search
prompt: A search `sourcetype=access_combined *checkout* | stats count` is scanning the entire index slowly. Rewrite it to be more efficient, and explain the key change.

correctAnswer.solution:
index=web sourcetype=access_combined "checkout" earliest=-1h latest=now
| stats count

explanation: Two changes matter most: adding an explicit `index=` so Splunk doesn't have to search every index the user has access to, and replacing the leading wildcard `*checkout*` with a plain term search `"checkout"` — leading wildcards defeat Splunk's indexed term lookup and force a much slower scan, plus a bounded time range avoids scanning the full retention period.

title: Exclude a field from results
prompt: Write a query that searches `sourcetype=access_combined` and shows all fields except `cookie`.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | fields - cookie

explanation: `| fields - fieldname` removes a specific field from the result set (as opposed to `| fields fieldname` which would keep only that field), useful for dropping noisy or sensitive fields before display.

title: Remove duplicate events by field
prompt: Write a query that returns one event per unique `user_id` from `sourcetype=login_events`, keeping only the most recent event for each user.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=login_events | sort -_time | dedup user_id

explanation: `dedup` keeps only the first event it sees for each unique value of the given field; sorting by `-_time` first ensures that "first seen" corresponds to "most recent", since dedup itself doesn't sort.

title: List distinct values per group
prompt: Write a query that shows, for each `host`, the distinct list of `status` codes seen.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | stats values(status) AS statuses by host

explanation: `values()` collects every distinct value of a field within each group into a multi-value result, unlike `list()` which keeps duplicates and preserves order.

title: Search with OR across sourcetypes
prompt: Write a query that returns events from either `sourcetype=app_log` or `sourcetype=db_log` that contain "timeout".

correctAnswer.solution: (sourcetype=app_log OR sourcetype=db_log) "timeout"

explanation: Parentheses group the OR condition so it's evaluated together before the implicit AND with the free-text "timeout" term — without them, operator precedence could produce a different, unintended filter.

title: First and last events in a search
prompt: Write two queries: one showing the 3 earliest events and one showing the 3 latest events from `sourcetype=deploy_log` today.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=deploy_log | sort _time | head 3

sourcetype=deploy_log | sort -_time | head 3

explanation: Splunk search results default to reverse-chronological order, so `sort _time` (ascending) then `head` gets the earliest events, while `sort -_time` (descending) then `head` gets the latest.

title: Search a specific date range
prompt: Write a query that searches `sourcetype=billing` events between July 1, 2026 and July 2, 2026.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=billing earliest=07/01/2026:00:00:00 latest=07/02/2026:00:00:00

explanation: `earliest=` and `latest=` accept absolute timestamps in `MM/DD/YYYY:HH:MM:SS` format when you need an exact date range instead of a relative one like `-24h`.

title: Cross-tabulate status by host
prompt: Write a query that produces a table with `host` as rows and `status` codes as columns, each cell showing the event count.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | chart count by host, status

explanation: `chart ... by field1, field2` cross-tabulates two dimensions into a table — the first field becomes rows and the second becomes columns — which `stats ... by` alone cannot do since stats only produces one row per group combination, not a pivoted grid.

title: Handle missing values before charting
prompt: Write a query that charts average `response_time` per 5-minute interval, ensuring any interval with no data shows 0 instead of a gap.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=access_combined | timechart span=5m avg(response_time) | fillnull value=0

explanation: `timechart` can leave gaps for buckets with no matching events; `fillnull value=0` replaces those null cells with 0 so charting libraries render a continuous line instead of breaking it.

title: Combine results from two different searches
prompt: Write a query that shows total order count alongside total revenue, computed from two different sourcetypes (`orders` and `payments`), as a single result row.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=orders | stats count AS order_count
| appendcols [search sourcetype=payments | stats sum(amount) AS total_revenue]

explanation: `appendcols` runs a second subsearch and adds its result columns side-by-side onto the first search's results (matched by row position, not by any join key) — useful when combining two independent aggregate numbers into one summary row.

title: Generate a static reference row
prompt: Write a query that creates a single synthetic result row with a field `threshold` set to 500, useful as a comparison baseline in a dashboard.

correctAnswer.solution: | makeresults | eval threshold=500

explanation: `makeresults` generates one empty result row with no underlying index search, which `eval` can then populate with computed or literal fields — commonly used to inject constants or reference lines into a dashboard panel.

title: Expand a multivalue field into separate events
prompt: Given events with a multivalue field `tags` (e.g. ["prod", "critical"]), write a query that produces one event per tag value.

correctAnswer.solution: sourcetype=incidents | mvexpand tags

explanation: `mvexpand` duplicates an event once per value in the specified multivalue field, turning a single event with 2 tag values into 2 separate events — necessary before you can `stats count by tags` and get a per-tag breakdown instead of counting the whole multivalue combination as one bucket.

title: Design an alert for repeated failures
prompt: Describe the SPL and trigger condition for a saved search alert that fires when more than 10 payment failures occur in a rolling 15-minute window.

correctAnswer.solution:
Search: sourcetype=payments status=failed
Schedule: run every 5 minutes, search range earliest=-15m
Trigger condition: Number of results > 10

explanation: Splunk alerts are a saved search plus a schedule plus a trigger condition evaluated on the search's result count (or a custom condition); running the search on a schedule shorter than the lookback window (5 min schedule, 15 min lookback) ensures overlapping windows don't miss a burst that happens to straddle a scheduled run boundary.

title: Flag consecutive failed logins per user
prompt: Write a query that flags a user as `is_brute_force=true` if they have 5 or more consecutive failed logins with no successful login in between, using streamstats.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=auth_log
| sort user, _time
| streamstats count AS consecutive_failures BY user RESET_ON action="success"
| where action="failure" AND consecutive_failures >= 5
| eval is_brute_force=true

explanation: `streamstats` computes a running aggregate event-by-event (unlike `stats`, which collapses to one row per group) and its `RESET_ON` clause restarts the count whenever a `success` event is seen for that user — precisely modeling "consecutive failures since the last success" rather than a total failure count across the whole search window.

title: Find events only for currently-active hosts
prompt: Given a lookup or search that identifies currently active hosts, write a query that filters `sourcetype=metrics` events to only those hosts, without hardcoding the host list.

correctAnswer.solution:
sourcetype=metrics
[ search sourcetype=host_inventory status=active | fields host | format ]

explanation: A bracketed subsearch runs first and its results are converted (via the implicit `format` command) into a search filter like `(host="web1" OR host="web2" OR ...)`, dynamically injected into the outer search — letting the active-host list come from live data instead of a hardcoded list that would go stale.

title: Explain when to use an accelerated data model over raw search
prompt: A dashboard runs the same `stats count by host, sourcetype` search over 30 days of data on every page load, and it's slow. Describe the fix using Splunk's data model acceleration, and why it helps.

correctAnswer.solution: Create a Splunk data model over the relevant event types, enable acceleration on it, and point the dashboard's search at the accelerated data model (via `| tstats count from datamodel=<model> by host, sourcetype`) instead of the raw `stats` search.

explanation: Data model acceleration pre-builds and maintains summary indexes (essentially materialized rollups) in the background, so `tstats` queries against an accelerated data model read from that pre-aggregated summary instead of re-scanning 30 days of raw events on every dashboard load — trading some indexing-time overhead for dramatically faster repeated query performance, which is the standard Splunk pattern for exactly this "slow recurring dashboard search" complaint.
